content:
# Leviticus Chapter 17 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Hebrew Base:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 68r–68v
**LXX Reference:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 103–104
**Disagreement Log:** `disagreements/LEV_017.md`

---

## Council Notes

Leviticus 17 stands at the structural hinge of the book — the closing chapter of the Priestly ritual code (chs. 1–16) and the opening of the Holiness Code (chs. 17–27 or 18–27, by various reckonings). Its three units are marked by the repeated distributive formula *ish ish* ("any man, any man"): (1) vv. 1–9, the centralization of all animal slaughter at the tent of meeting, with the karet penalty for violations and the alarming identification of field-slaughter with bloodguilt equivalent to murder; (2) vv. 10–14, the blood prohibition grounded in the chapter's theological center — *ki nefesh ha-basar ba-dam hi* ("for the life of the flesh is in the blood," v. 11) — stated three times in varied form (vv. 11, 14a, 14b); (3) vv. 15–16, the case of the *nevalah* (naturally dead animal) and *treifah* (torn animal), which incurs impurity but not the karet penalty. The chapter's extraordinary theological density flows from verse 11, which supplies the atonement-logic for the entire sacrificial system: blood is the material vehicle of *nefesh*; *nefesh* given on the altar covers the offerer's *nefesh*; God himself is the donor of this provision ("and I — I have given it to you"). This statement is foundational to Hebrews' theology of blood and Paul's "justified by his blood" (Romans 5:9).

The mention of *se'irim* (goat-demons, v. 7) is not hypothetical — the Hebrew present participle (*zonim*, "going a-whoring") marks this as ongoing practice being prohibited, not a merely theoretical temptation. The LXX's flattening of *se'irim* to *mataia* ("vain things") demythologizes where the Hebrew is specific; the consensus follows the MT. The inclusion of the *ger* (sojourner/resident alien) at every major prohibition (vv. 8, 10, 12, 13, 15) signals that the blood-theology is cosmological, not merely ethnic: *nefesh* in blood is a property of all living creatures, and its disposal belongs to God alone.

**Key term rulings:** *chattat* = "sin offering" | *olah* = "burnt offering" | *shelamim* = "peace offerings" | YHWH = "the LORD" | *bney yisrael* = "sons of Israel" | *se'irim* = "goat-demons" (with gloss) | *nefesh* = "life" (in the blood-theology statements) / "person" (in legal person-formulas) | *karet/nikrat* = "cut off" | *nevalah* = "carcass" | *treifah* = "torn animal" | *dam yechashev* = "bloodguilt shall be reckoned" | English name forms throughout

---

## Consensus Translation

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying,

**2** "Speak to Aaron and to his sons and to all the sons of Israel, and say to them: This is the word that the LORD has commanded, saying:

**3** Any man of the house of Israel who slaughters an ox or a lamb or a goat — whether in the camp or outside the camp —

**4** and does not bring it to the entrance of the tent of meeting to offer it as an offering to the LORD before the tabernacle of the LORD: bloodguilt shall be reckoned to that man — he has shed blood — and that man shall be cut off from among his people.

**5** This is so that the sons of Israel may bring their sacrifices that they sacrifice in the open field, and bring them to the LORD, to the entrance of the tent of meeting, to the priest, and sacrifice them as peace offerings to the LORD.

**6** And the priest shall dash the blood against the altar of the LORD at the entrance of the tent of meeting, and burn the fat as a soothing aroma to the LORD.

**7** So they shall no longer sacrifice their sacrifices to the goat-demons [*se'irim*], after whom they are going a-whoring. This shall be a perpetual statute for them throughout their generations.

**8** "And you shall say to them: Any man of the house of Israel, or of the sojourners who sojourn among them, who offers a burnt offering or a sacrifice,

**9** and does not bring it to the entrance of the tent of meeting to offer it to the LORD — that man shall be cut off from his people.

**10** "And any man of the house of Israel, or of the sojourners who sojourn among them, who eats any blood — I will set my face against that person who eats blood, and I will cut him off from among his people.

**11** For the life [*nefesh*] of the flesh is in the blood, and I myself have given it to you upon the altar to make atonement for your lives [*nafshoteikhem*] — for it is the blood that makes atonement by the life [*ba-nefesh*].

**12** Therefore I have said to the sons of Israel: No person among you shall eat blood, and the sojourner who sojourns among you shall not eat blood.

**13** "And any man of the sons of Israel, or of the sojourners who sojourn among them, who hunts game — a wild animal or a bird that may be eaten — he shall pour out its blood and cover it with dust.

**14** For the life [*nefesh*] of every creature is its blood — its blood is its life [*be-nafsho*]. Therefore I have said to the sons of Israel: You shall not eat the blood of any creature, for the life of every creature is its blood. Anyone who eats it shall be cut off.

**15** "And every person — whether native or sojourner — who eats a carcass [*nevalah*] or a torn animal [*treifah*] shall wash his garments and bathe in water, and he shall be unclean until the evening; then he shall be clean.

**16** But if he does not wash and his body is not bathed, he shall bear his iniquity."

---

## Verse-by-Verse Ruling Notes

**V. 1 — Name form.** EMET: "Moshe." LOGOS/PNEUMA: "Moses." Consensus: "Moses" — English name form, project convention throughout.

**V. 2 — Address formula structure.** PNEUMA merges vv. 1–2 into a single run-on and renders "all the Israelites" for *bney Yisra'el*. LOGOS: "sons of Israel." EMET: "sons of Yisra'el." Consensus: "sons of Israel" — the project convention retains the formal address *bney Yisra'el* as "sons of Israel" throughout (cf. SOPHIA's ruling at Lev 12:2). PNEUMA's "Israelites" is natural but loses the generational-familial resonance of *bney*. The two-level speech structure (God to Moses; Moses to Israel) is preserved in the consensus against PNEUMA's compression.

**V. 2 — *davar* — "word" vs. "thing" vs. "command."** EMET: "This is the thing." LOGOS: "This is the word." PNEUMA: "this is what the LORD has ordered." Consensus: "This is the word" — *davar* means both "word" and "thing/matter"; in the formula *zeh ha-davar asher tzivah YHWH* it functions as a formal promulgation: "This is the word that the LORD has commanded." LOGOS's "word" captures the speech-act register better than EMET's "thing" and is far preferable to PNEUMA's loose paraphrase "what the LORD has ordered" which dissolves the formula.

**V. 3 — *shor* — "ox" vs. "bull."** EMET: "bull." LOGOS: "ox." PNEUMA: "ox." Consensus: "ox" — *shor* is the standard bovine term, most naturally rendered "ox" in a sacrificial/livestock context (referring to any bovine of either sex). EMET's "bull" implies a male specifically; "ox" is the broader and more accurate rendering here. LOGOS and PNEUMA agree; ruling follows them.

**V. 3 — *kesev* — "lamb" vs. "sheep."** EMET: "lamb." LOGOS: "lamb." PNEUMA: "sheep." Consensus: "lamb" — *kesev* is the sheep of sacrificial age, most precisely "lamb." PNEUMA's "sheep" is too general. EMET and LOGOS agree; ruling follows them.

**V. 3 — "Any man of the house" vs. "Any Israelite."** EMET: "Any man from the house of Yisra'el." LOGOS: "Any man of the house of Israel." PNEUMA: "Any Israelite." Consensus: "Any man of the house of Israel" — the doubled *ish ish* ("a man, a man") and *mi-beit Yisra'el* are technical legal formulas. PNEUMA's "Any Israelite" is too compressed: it loses the distributive *ish ish* and the corporate "house" designation. LOGOS's rendering is correct; EMET's is materially identical with transliterations replaced by English forms.

**V. 4 — *dam yechashev* — "bloodguilt reckoned" vs. "blood shall be imputed" vs. "guilty of bloodshed."** EMET: "blood shall be imputed to that man." LOGOS: "bloodguilt shall be reckoned to that man." PNEUMA: "that man is guilty of bloodshed." Consensus: "bloodguilt shall be reckoned to that man" — LOGOS's rendering is the most precise: *dam* ("blood") + *yechashev* (niphal of *chashav*, "shall be reckoned/imputed") creates a forensic declaration. "Bloodguilt" as a compound noun accurately conveys that the blood of the animal is charged to the slaughterer as a capital crime. PNEUMA's "guilty of bloodshed" is adequate but loses the juridical precision of *chashav* (the reckoning/imputation verb). EMET's "blood shall be imputed" is also acceptable but "bloodguilt" conveys the compound concept more clearly.

**V. 4 — Order of clauses.** PNEUMA omits the phrase "to offer it as an offering to the LORD before the tabernacle of the LORD" entirely, collapsing the verse significantly. EMET and LOGOS both include the full structure: the condition (not bringing to the tent), the positive purpose (to offer before the tabernacle), the verdict (bloodguilt reckoned), the reason (he has shed blood), the penalty (cut off). The consensus retains all clauses in the MT order, following EMET and LOGOS against PNEUMA's omission.

**V. 5 — "open field" vs. "open country."** EMET/LOGOS: "open field." PNEUMA: "open country." Consensus: "open field" — *ha-sadeh* is "the field, the open land." "Open field" is the more natural and precise English equivalent. "Open country" (PNEUMA) is not wrong but is slightly more expansive. EMET and LOGOS agree; ruling follows them.

**V. 5 — *shelamim* — "peace offerings" vs. "fellowship offerings."** EMET/LOGOS: "peace offerings." PNEUMA: "fellowship offerings." Consensus: "peace offerings" — the terminology ruling for this project specifies *shelamim* = "peace offerings." PNEUMA's "fellowship offerings" (the NIV rendering) is not the project standard. EMET and LOGOS agree; ruling follows project convention.

**V. 6 — *zaraq* — "dash" vs. "throw" vs. "splash."** EMET: "dash." LOGOS: "throw." PNEUMA: "splash." Consensus: "dash" — *zaraq* is the technical term for the action of throwing blood from a bowl against the altar sides or base. "Dash" captures both the force and the directed movement of the action better than "throw" (too casual) or "splash" (too incidental). EMET's "dash" is the standard rendering throughout the sacrificial vocabulary in this translation (cf. Lev 1:5, 11; 3:2, 8, 13).

**V. 6 — *reyach nichoach* — "soothing aroma" vs. "pleasing aroma."** EMET: "soothing aroma." LOGOS/PNEUMA: "pleasing aroma." Consensus: "soothing aroma" — *reyach nichoach* = "aroma of rest/soothing" (*nichoach* from *nuach*, "to rest"). "Soothing aroma" preserves the specific semantic root of *nichoach* (rest, settling, soothing) better than "pleasing," which is a more generic positive evaluation. The formula *reyach nichoach la-YHWH* is used over 40 times in Leviticus; the consensus maintains "soothing aroma" for consistency with the pattern established in Leviticus 1–16.

**V. 7 — *zonim* — "going a-whoring" vs. "playing the harlot" vs. "prostituting themselves."** EMET: "going a-whoring." LOGOS: "playing the harlot." PNEUMA: "prostituting themselves." Consensus: "going a-whoring" — the *qal* participle *zonim* with *achareihem* ("after them") is the classic prophetic idiom for covenant apostasy expressed as sexual faithlessness. EMET's "going a-whoring" preserves both the participle's continuous aspect ("keep going") and the strong register of the Hebrew root. LOGOS's "playing the harlot" is acceptable but slightly formal; PNEUMA's "prostituting themselves to" is accurate but slightly different in its directional construction. "Going a-whoring after them" preserves the *zonim achareihem* structure most faithfully.

**V. 7 — *chuqqat olam* — "perpetual statute" vs. "statute forever" vs. "permanent statute."** EMET: "perpetual statute." LOGOS: "a statute forever." PNEUMA: "permanent statute." Consensus: "perpetual statute" — *chuqqat olam* = "statute of eternity / perpetual statute." EMET's "perpetual statute" is compact, precise, and consistent with the project's rendering of this formula throughout Leviticus. LOGOS's "a statute forever" is accurate but verbose; PNEUMA's "permanent statute" is slightly weaker (*olam* = eternity, not merely permanence).

**V. 8 — "sojourners" vs. "foreigners."** EMET/LOGOS: "the sojourners who sojourn among them." PNEUMA: "any foreigner living among you." Consensus: "the sojourners who sojourn among them" — *ha-ger ha-gar be-tokham* preserves the cognate verbal construction (the sojourner-who-sojourns) that EMET and LOGOS maintain throughout the chapter. PNEUMA's "foreigner living among you" is an acceptable dynamic rendering but loses the *ger* technical term that will recur in vv. 10, 12, 13, 15 and throughout the Holiness Code. Consistency demands the formal equivalent throughout.

**V. 9 — "that man" vs. "that person."** EMET/LOGOS: "that man." PNEUMA: "that person." Consensus: "that man" — *ha-ish ha-hu'* is "that man," using *ish* (the masculine singular). While the sojourner may be of any gender, the Hebrew formula uses *ish* throughout this chapter. The formal legal idiom is retained in the consensus. Note: PNEUMA's shift to gender-neutral "that person" at this verse (but not consistently throughout) is an ad hoc departure from the formula; the project follows formal equivalence for legal idioms.

**V. 10 — "cut him off" vs. "cut them off."** EMET/LOGOS: "cut him off." PNEUMA: "cut them off." Consensus: "cut him off" — the Hebrew suffix is masculine singular (*oto*, "him"), referring back to the individual *nefesh* who eats blood. PNEUMA's plural "them" is a gender-neutral accommodation not present in the Hebrew.

**V. 11 — "upon the altar" vs. "on the altar."** EMET: "upon the altar." LOGOS/PNEUMA: "on the altar." Consensus: "upon the altar" — *al-ha-mizbeath*: *al* is "upon/over," the standard spatial preposition for altar-application. "Upon" better preserves the directional sense of the blood being placed on the altar surface.

**V. 11 — "I myself have given" vs. "I have given."** EMET: "I have given it." LOGOS: "I have given it to you." PNEUMA: "I myself have given it." Consensus: "I myself have given it" — *va-ani netattiv* fronts the emphatic personal pronoun *ani* ("I"), a grammatical signal of special emphasis. "I myself" captures this emphasis, which is theologically crucial: God is the donor of the atoning blood, not the recipient of a human initiative. PNEUMA correctly preserves this emphasis; the consensus follows.

**V. 12 — "No person" vs. "No one."** EMET/LOGOS: "No person among you." PNEUMA: "No one among you." Consensus: "No person among you" — *kol-nefesh mi-kem* = "any *nefesh* from among you," i.e., any living person. The use of *nefesh* here is significant — the same word used in v. 11 for "life" now designates the individual person. The word "person" preserves this continuity; "no one" (PNEUMA) is idiomatic but loses the *nefesh* connection.

**V. 13 — "wild animal" vs. "beast."** EMET: "a wild animal or a bird." LOGOS: "beast or bird." PNEUMA: "an animal or a bird." Consensus: "a wild animal or a bird" — *chayah* is the category of wild animals (as distinct from *beheimah*, domesticated livestock). "Wild animal" is precise. LOGOS's "beast" is archaic; PNEUMA's bare "animal" loses the wild/domestic distinction that is the point of the verse (hunted game cannot be brought to the altar). EMET's "wild animal" is correct.

**V. 13 — *afar* — "dust" vs. "dirt."** EMET/LOGOS: "dust." PNEUMA: "dirt." Consensus: "dust" — *afar* = "dust, loose earth" (the same word as Gen 2:7, 3:19: from dust Adam was formed and to dust he returns). "Dust" preserves the semantic and theological resonance of *afar*. "Dirt" is a casual equivalent that loses the cosmological echo.

**V. 14 — "I have said" vs. "I said" vs. "And so I said."** EMET: "I said" (narrative *va-omar*). LOGOS: "I have said." PNEUMA: "And so I said." Consensus: "I have said" — the waw-consecutive *va-omar* introduces a past divine declaration. Rendering it as "I have said" (perfect tense) makes clear this is a standing decree with present force, not merely a past event. PNEUMA's "And so I said" is slightly loose; LOGOS's "I have said" is the most natural and accurate.

**V. 15 — *nevalah* and *treifah* — carcass vs. died naturally / torn by beasts.** EMET: "a carcass or torn [animal]." LOGOS: "what has died naturally or what was torn by beasts." PNEUMA: "an animal that died on its own or was torn by wild beasts." Consensus: "a carcass [*nevalah*] or a torn animal [*treifah*]" — these are foundational technical terms that deserve transliteration alongside a gloss at their first conjunction. *Nevalah* (the technical term for a naturally-dead animal, whose blood has not been drained) is rendered "carcass" with the Hebrew in brackets, following EMET's precision. *Treifah* (torn by a wild animal, source of the later Yiddish "treif") is rendered "torn animal" with the Hebrew in brackets. LOGOS's definitional rendering is clear but loses the technical terms; PNEUMA's expansive "died on its own" is accurate but overly explanatory. The bracketed Hebrew terms are retained per project convention for key technical vocabulary (cf. *se'irim*, *niddah*, *nevalah*).

**V. 15 — "native" vs. "native-born."** EMET/LOGOS: "native." PNEUMA: "native-born." Consensus: "native" — *ha-ezrach* = "the native" (one who has sprung up in the land). "Native" is the standard project rendering (cf. Lev 16:29; the ezrach/ger pair throughout). "Native-born" (PNEUMA) adds "born" not in the Hebrew.

**V. 16 — "bear his iniquity" vs. "bear the consequences of their sin."** EMET/LOGOS: "bear his iniquity." PNEUMA: "bear the consequences of their sin." Consensus: "bear his iniquity" — *nasa' avono* = "to carry/bear his iniquity"; the formula *nasa' avon* appears consistently throughout Leviticus (cf. chs. 5, 7, 19, 20, 22) as the technical expression for personal accountability for unresolved guilt. PNEUMA's "consequences of their sin" — while explanatory — dissolves the formula, shifts to the plural (not in the Hebrew), and replaces *avon* ("iniquity") with "sin" (a different category). The technical formula is retained in the consensus, following EMET and LOGOS.

---

## Textual Notes for the Consensus Record

**On the LXX rendering of *se'irim* (v. 7):** The Vaticanus renders *la-se'irim* ("to the goat-demons") as *tois mataiois* ("to the vain/worthless things") — a deliberate demythologizing that reduces the *se'irim* to mere idols rather than actual demonic entities. All three agents correctly render "goat-demons," following the MT's specific and startling designation of demonic wilderness-spirits (cf. Isaiah 13:21, 34:14; 2 Chronicles 11:15). The LXX polemical rendering is noted but not followed.

**On *dam yechashev* and Romans 4 (v. 4):** The verb *chashav* (niphal: "shall be reckoned/imputed") applied to *dam* creates a forensic declaration that unauthorized slaughter constitutes bloodguilt equivalent to homicide. The LXX renders this with *logizomai* ("to reckon, to impute") — the same verb Paul uses in Romans 4 for the imputation of righteousness. The verb's theological reach from Leviticus 17 to the Pauline letters is one of the most instructive lexical chains in biblical theology.

**On the structure of v. 11:** The verse unfolds in three movements: (1) *ki nefesh ha-basar ba-dam hi* — the ontological claim: the animating life-force (*nefesh*) of flesh is located in blood; (2) *va-ani netattiv lakhem al-ha-mizbeath le-khapper al-nafshoteikhem* — the theological reversal: God himself is the donor of the atoning blood, not the recipient of a human tribute; (3) *ki ha-dam hu ba-nefesh yekhapper* — the mechanism: blood atones through the *nefesh* it contains, a life given for a life. The LXX at this point adds *anti tēs psychēs* ("in exchange for the life") — making the substitutionary logic explicit with *anti* ("in place of") where the MT uses the instrumental *be-* ("by means of"). The MT's *be-nefesh* ("by/through the life") is retained in the consensus; the LXX's interpretive expansion is noted.

**On the triple iteration of the blood prohibition:** The blood prohibition appears three times (vv. 10, 12, 14) — a rhetorical intensification unique in this chapter. The triple repetition mirrors the triple deployment of the karet penalty (vv. 4, 9, 14), making the blood prohibition the most emphatically enforced regulation in the chapter. The structure is intentional and is preserved in the consensus against PNEUMA's tendency to compress or paraphrase repeated formulas.

**On the inclusion of the *ger* at every prohibition (vv. 8, 10, 12, 13, 15):** The consistent inclusion of the sojourner in every major clause signals that the blood-theology is grounded in creation, not covenant particularity. *Nefesh* is the property of every living creature; therefore its disposal belongs to God for all persons within the community, not only native Israelites. This universalizing logic anticipates the Noahide blood prohibition of Genesis 9:4–6.

---

*SOPHIA consensus completed. Disagreements logged in `disagreements/LEV_017.md`.*
